Ice Breaker / Motivation

Ice Breaker 1: The Multi-Role

Teaser: "I am the person in the room who has to be a manager, a leader, a parent, a counselor, and a teacher all at the same time. Who am I, and can you name one task I do for each role?"

Answer: The Teacher.

Teaching Focus: This engages students in discussing the varied roles of the teacher, which is a core component of the teaching-learning process.

Ice Breaker 2: The "Hidden Learner" Challenge

Teaser: "Look at these four words: TEACHER, LEARNER, ENVIRONMENT, SCHOOL. Count how many total letters there are across all four words combined. You have 10 seconds!"

Answer: 30 letters.

Teaching Focus: This builds anticipation for the three main elements of the teaching-learning process (Teacher, Learner, Environment) while getting students to focus intently on the screen.

Ice Breaker 3: Word Scramble

Teaser: "I am the type of intelligence that helps you find the 'right words' to express exactly what you mean. I am called '____ Smart.' Unscramble these letters to find me: I-L-U-N-G-I-S-T-I-C."

Answer: Linguistic.

Teaching Focus: This is a fun introduction to Multiple Intelligences, specifically getting students to think about their own strengths before you dive into the theory.

Motivation

Philippians 4:13

"I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me."

Recap of Previous Lesson

Objectives of the Lesson

Objectives

  • Understand the core elements of the teaching-learning process.
  • Identify the various roles of a teacher in a classroom.
  • Explore different learning styles and multiple intelligences.
  • Apply Thorndike's Laws of Learning and other learning theories.

The Teaching-Learning Process

Elements of Teaching -Learning Process

Teaching and learning involves the process of transferring knowledge from the one who is giving to the one who is receiving. Teaching process cannot be performed if there is one element that is missing among the three of the teaching and learning elements.

There is what we called as elements of teaching and learning processes. These elements are necessary to be able to make teaching and learning possible. Without one of these elements, there could be no real teaching or learning process that will exist. It is so important that the presence of these elements is present in the process of teaching, considering that all of them play an important role in the system The elements of teaching and learning process are the teacher, the leaner as well as the good learning environment. It is being considered that learning occur when there is established relationship among these three elements. The teaching as well as the learning activity depends upon how these elements works together.

The Teacher

The teacher is considered as the element that has the main role in the teaching-learning process. He/she is considered as the so-called prime mover of the educational processes, thus he she directs the flow of the whole process. The teacher is the one that facilitates the whole process of leaning. He or she directs its flow and serve as main control of the teaching learning process.

The Learners

The learners are considered as the key participant in the teaching and learning process. They are considered as the primary subject or the main reason why the process is implemented. The knowledge that acquired by the learners will decide if the teaching and learning objectives are achieved. Learners vary from one another in the aspects of learning. There are those learners that learn fast while there are those learners that learn in average or slower.

The Enviroment

The favorable environment, participates in the teaching-learning process by providing a place where there is a smooth flow of communication, avoiding some common barriers between the teacher and the learner. The presence of a good environment is so much important in the teaching and learning process. The good environment provides a smooth flow of communication between the learners and the teachers, thus it facilitates a well executed teaching and learning process. A good environment is necessary for learning. The reason why we should make sure that we should have this kind of environment, as we teach or we learn.

Roles of the Teacher

  • Teacher as Manager: Systematic classroom order.
  • Teacher as Leader: Director, coach, and supporter.
  • Teacher as Surrogate Parent: Providing security and emotional well-being.
  • Teacher as Counselor: Guidance figure for personal challenges.
  • Teacher as Model: Exemplar for behavior and judgment.
  • Teacher as Public Relations Specialist: External stakeholder management.
  • Teacher as Facilitator & Instructor: Guiding core instruction.

Visual Learning Style

The Learning Environment & Visual/Aural Learning Styles

Presented by: (Alexandrei Raymundo)

First – What is the Learning Environment?

• Refers to the classroom or surroundings where students learn.

• Includes teachers, classmates, facilities, and learning atmosphere.

• A positive environment improves focus, motivation, and participation.

Visual Learning Style

• Learns by seeing

• Prefers pictures, charts, graphs, videos, and diagrams

• Fast talkers

• Can easily visualize ideas

• Teaching Tip: Use charts, graphs, and visual aids

Aural Learning Style

• Learns by hearing

• Prefers lectures and discussions

• Natural listeners

• Slow speakers

• Teaching Tip: Use verbal explanations and discussions

Lastly in Conclusion

• Students have different learning styles.

• A positive learning environment supports learning.

• Combining visual and aural teaching methods benefits all students.

Aural Learning Style

learning styles

Read/Write

• Prefer written text

• Emphasize text-based input and output

• Enjoy reading and writing

Read/Write

Kinesthetic

• Slowest talkers

• Slow to decide

• Use all senses to engage in learning

Do and solve

• Prefer hands-on approaches

• Learn through trial and error

multiple intelligence

1. Spatial Intelligence- these learners think in images and are called "Picture Smart”

2. Musical Intelligence - these are the musicians and singers of our world called “Music Smart”

3. Linguistic Intelligence - having a way with words, these are called "Word Smart" (both in speech and writing)

4. Bodily-kinesthetic Intelligence - These learners have good body control like physical exercise and working with their hands. They use a lot of gestures while talking and are called "Body Smart"

5. Logical- mathematical intelligence - These learners excel at Science and Math and are called "Number Smart"

6. interpersonal Intelligence - These are people persons. They like to be around people and are called "People Smart"

7. intrapersonal Intelligence - Learners with intrapersonal intelligence are very introspective and independent. They like being alone and are called

"Self-Smart".

8. Natural Intelligence - observing, understanding, and organizing patterns in the natural environment. They are called "Nature Smart".

Read/Write Learning Style

The Law of Readiness

The "Get Ready" Rule

  • Learning starts with your mindset. When you are mentally prepared and genuinely motivated to learn, the entire process feels natural and rewarding. However, if you are forced to learn something when you are tired, distracted, or unwilling, the experience becomes frustrating and ineffective. Simply put, you have to be "in the zone" to make learning stick.

The Law of Exercise

The "Practice Makes Improvement" Rule

  • This law is all about the power of repetition. Every time you practice a skill or repeat an action, your brain strengthens the connection for that task, making it easier and more automatic over time. On the other hand, if you stop practicing, that connection fades away, and the skill eventually becomes "rusty." Consistency is the key to mastery.

The Law of Effect

The "Repeat the Winners" Rule

  • We are naturally wired to repeat actions that lead to good outcomes. When a behavior is followed by a reward, a win, or a feeling of success, your brain logs that as a positive experience, making you much more likely to repeat it in the future. Conversely, if an action leads to a bad result or failure, we instinctively learn to avoid that path. We stick with what works.

Kinesthetic Learning Style

Secondary Laws of Thorndikes

  • LAW OF BELONGINGNESS (natural association of stimuli)

Learning is faster and stronger when the learner perceives a natural connection between stimuli involved.

  • LAW OF INTENSITY (strength of experience)

An experience that is vivid, dramatic, or striking is learned and remembered more deeply than an ordinary one.

  • LAW OF FORGETTING (memory linked to usage)

Skills and knowledge that are not used or reviewed over time gradually fade from memory.

Multiple Intelligences Part 1

Operant Conditioning

It is a type of learning where a person must actively do something to its environment to produce a result or gain a reward.

Behaviorism

This theory completely denies the existence of inborn tendencies or instincts. Instead, it assumes that learning is a process of building reflexes and habits solely through interaction with our surroundings.

Skinner's Operant Conditioning (The Experiment)

This is B.F. Skinner’s specific learning process theory that highlights how environmental conditions directly bring about behavioral changes.

Types of Operant Conditioning

Primary Reward Conditioning: Learning that is instrumental in getting a reward that is biologically significant, like food or water.

Secondary Reward Conditioning: Learning where the behavior gets a reward that has no immediate biological utility but is associated with value, like money, praise, or a good report card.

Escape Conditioning: Learning a response that allows an organism to get out of an unpleasant or uncomfortable place or situation that is already happening.

Avoidance Conditioning: Learning a behavior in response to a warning cue or signal to completely avoid or prevent a painful or unpleasant experience before it even happens.

Multiple Intelligences Part 2

Cognitive Perspectives and Intelligence Theories

Cognitive Perspective

The Cognitive Perspective is a theory of learning that focuses on the mental processes involved in acquiring knowledge. Unlike behaviorism, which emphasizes observable behavior, the cognitive perspective explains that learning occurs through internal mental activities such as thinking, remembering, reasoning, problem-solving, decision-making, understanding, and perception. Cognitive psychologists believe that the human mind works similarly to a computer. It receives information from the environment, processes it, stores it in memory, and retrieves it whenever needed. Because of this, learning is not simply a change in behavior but also a change in the way people think, understand, and organize information.

For example, when a student reads a lesson, the brain does not simply memorize the words. Instead, it interprets their meaning, connects them with previous knowledge, and stores them in memory for future use. This explains why understanding a lesson is more effective than memorizing it without comprehension.

Gestalt Theory

Gestalt Theory is one of the major theories under the Cognitive Perspective. It was developed by German psychologists who believed that people naturally perceive objects and situations as complete wholes rather than separate parts. The word "Gestalt" is a German term that means "whole" or "complete form." The main principle of this theory is "The whole is greater than the sum of its parts."

This means that when people observe something, the brain automatically organizes individual pieces of information into one meaningful whole. Instead of analyzing every detail separately, the mind first recognizes the overall pattern or image.

For example, when you see a bicycle, your brain immediately recognizes it as a bicycle. You do not first identify the wheels, handlebars, pedals, and seat before understanding what it is. Your brain combines all these parts into one complete object.

Gestalt Theory also explains several principles of perception:

Similarity refers to the tendency of the brain to group objects that look alike. Objects with the same color, shape, or size are naturally seen as belonging together.

Proximity explains that objects placed close to one another are perceived as part of the same group.

Closure means that the brain naturally fills in missing parts of an incomplete image. Even if a drawing has gaps, people still recognize the complete object.

Continuity states that the eyes naturally follow smooth and continuous lines instead of broken or disconnected patterns.

These principles help explain how people organize and understand visual information quickly and efficiently.

Field Theory (Kurt Lewin)

Field Theory was developed by Kurt Lewin. This theory explains that learning and behavior are influenced by both the individual and the environment. Lewin believed that behavior cannot be explained by personal characteristics alone because the environment also plays a significant role.

He summarized this idea using the formula:

Behavior (B) = Person (P) + Environment (E)

This means that a person's actions are affected by who they are and by the surroundings in which they live or learn.

For example, imagine two students preparing for an examination. One studies in a quiet library, while the other studies in a noisy place. Even if both students are equally intelligent, the student in the quiet environment is more likely to concentrate and learn effectively. This example shows that a positive learning environment can improve academic performance.

For teachers, Field Theory emphasizes the importance of creating a classroom that is safe, organized, supportive, and free from unnecessary distractions.

Intelligence Theories

Intelligence refers to the ability to think, reason, solve problems, understand ideas, learn from experience, and adapt to new situations. Different psychologists have proposed different explanations of intelligence. Two of the most influential theories were developed by Alfred Binet and Charles Spearman.

Alfred Binet's Theory of Intelligence

Alfred Binet is widely known as the Father of Intelligence Testing because he developed the first practical intelligence test. His goal was to identify students who needed additional educational support.

Binet believed that intelligence is the ability to reason, understand, solve problems, make good decisions, and adapt to different situations. He also believed that intelligence is not fixed and can improve through proper education, practice, and learning experiences.

For example, if two students are given a difficult mathematical problem, the student who understands how to solve the problem instead of simply memorizing formulas demonstrates intelligence according to Binet's theory.

Binet's theory reminds teachers that every learner has the potential to improve through effective instruction and continuous learning.

Charles Spearman's Theory of Intelligence

Charles Spearman introduced the Theory of General Intelligence, commonly known as the g-factor Theory. He believed that every person possesses one general mental ability called the g-factor, which influences performance in many different intellectual tasks.

According to Spearman, students with a strong general intelligence often perform well in different academic subjects because the same mental ability supports various kinds of thinking.

He also proposed the s-factor, which refers to specific abilities or talents unique to certain tasks. While a person may have strong general intelligence, they may also possess special talents in areas such as music, art, sports, or language.

For example, a student who consistently performs well in mathematics, science, and logical reasoning may have a strong g-factor. Another student may be exceptionally talented in music or painting because of strong specific abilities.

Spearman's theory explains why people may share overall intellectual ability while still having different strengths and talents.

Comparison of Binet and Spearman

Although both psychologists studied intelligence, they explained it differently. Alfred Binet believed that intelligence is mainly the ability to reason, solve problems, and adapt to situations. He also believed that intelligence can improve through education and experience.

Charles Spearman, on the other hand, believed that intelligence consists of one general mental ability called the g-factor that affects performance across many different tasks. He also recognized specific abilities, known as the s-factor, which explain why individuals have unique talents.

Importance of Cognitive Perspectives and Intelligence Theories in Education

Understanding Cognitive Perspectives and Intelligence Theories helps teachers become more effective educators. These theories remind teachers that learning is an active mental process and that students learn differently depending on how they think, understand, and interact with their environment. They also emphasize that intelligence is not simply about memorizing facts but involves reasoning, understanding, and solving problems.

As future teachers, it is important to recognize that every learner has different abilities, strengths, and learning needs. By understanding these theories, educators can create lessons that encourage critical thinking, meaningful learning, and equal opportunities for every student to succeed.

Assessment

Let's review what we've learned!

Question 1

The systematic interaction among the teacher, learner, and environment.

Teaching-Learning Process

Question 2

A learning style in which students learn best through physical activities.

Kinesthetic Learning Style

Question 3

The law stating that learning is strengthened through rewards.

Law of Effect

Question 4

The theory stating that behavior is influenced by both the individual and the environment.

Field Theory

Question 5

The theory explaining that people naturally perceive objects as complete wholes.

Gestalt Theory

Proverbs 1:5

"Let the wise listen and add to their learning, and let the discerning get guidance."
